content.text: Workflows vs Agents: The Core Tradeoff

A workflow is a directed graph of LLM calls and deterministic code steps where the execution path is known before the input arrives. An agent, by contrast, decides at runtime which steps to take and which tools to invoke. Workflows are faster, cheaper, and easier to test precisely because they give up adaptability. Choosing workflows means choosing predictability over flexibility — and that is often the right trade.

attrs.alt: Decision guide for when to choose workflows
attrs.title: When to Use Workflows

content.text: Defining Characteristics of Workflows
A workflow has three structural properties that together define it: a
fixed graph
(nodes and edges are defined at build time, not chosen at runtime),
deterministic routing
(branches are taken based on output values, not on the LLM choosing which tool to call next), and
bounded tool use

(if tools are used at all, they are called at specific nodes, not selected dynamically from a registry). Any design that satisfies these three properties is a workflow, regardless of how many LLM calls it makes or how complex its branching logic is.
The Five Signals That Point to a Workflow
Use a workflow when the task shows one or more of these properties:
The steps are known in advance.

You can draw the full DAG on a whiteboard before writing any code. Each node is a single LLM call, a data transformation, or an external API call. No node decides which node comes next based on open-ended reasoning.
Output format is fully specified.
The caller expects a particular schema — a JSON object, a structured report, a classified label. The workflow can enforce this at each step with Pydantic and
with_structured_output
, guaranteeing contract compliance regardless of input variation.
Latency or cost is a hard constraint.

Agents retry, backtrack, and call multiple tools to explore, multiplying LLM calls unpredictably. A workflow makes exactly as many LLM calls as the graph has nodes, giving a predictable cost envelope and a stable p99 latency.
Auditability is required.

Regulated industries, legal review, and medical applications demand that every transformation be traceable. A workflow's fixed graph means you can log every node's input and output and reproduce the exact execution trace.
The task is well-understood and stable.

If the domain logic has not changed in six months and is unlikely to change, encoding it in a workflow's graph structure is safer than delegating decisions to an agent. Agents are useful when requirements are uncertain; workflows are the right choice once they are clear.
A Concrete Workflow: Document Triage Pipeline
This pipeline classifies an incoming document, extracts the relevant fields for its category, and formats a structured report — three fixed steps, no dynamic tool selection, fully reproducible.

from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate
from typing import Literal

llm = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini", temperature=0)

# Step 1 — classify
class DocClass(BaseModel):
    category: Literal["invoice", "contract", "support_ticket", "other"]
    confidence: float = Field(ge=0.0, le=1.0)

classify_prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([
    ("system", "Classify the document type into exactly one category."),
    ("human", "{text}")
])
classify_chain = classify_prompt | llm.with_structured_output(DocClass)

# Step 2 — extract (schema varies by category)
class InvoiceFields(BaseModel):
    vendor: str
    total_amount: float
    due_date: str
    line_items: list[str]

class ContractFields(BaseModel):
    parties: list[str]
    effective_date: str
    expiry_date: str
    governing_law: str

def make_extract_chain(schema: type[BaseModel]) -> object:
    prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([
        ("system", "Extract structured fields from the document. Use null for missing values."),
        ("human", "{text}")
    ])
    return prompt | llm.with_structured_output(schema)

# Step 3 — triage runner (the workflow graph)
def triage_document(text: str) -> dict:
    # Node 1: classify
    doc_class = classify_chain.invoke({"text": text})

    # Node 2: branch (deterministic — based on output value, not LLM choice)
    if doc_class.category == "invoice":
        fields = make_extract_chain(InvoiceFields).invoke({"text": text})
    elif doc_class.category == "contract":
        fields = make_extract_chain(ContractFields).invoke({"text": text})
    else:
        fields = {"raw": text[:500]}  # fallback: no extraction

    # Node 3: assemble report
    return {
        "category": doc_class.category,
        "confidence": doc_class.confidence,
        "fields": fields.model_dump() if hasattr(fields, "model_dump") else fields,
    }

# Usage
result = triage_document("Invoice #1042 from Acme Corp, total $4,200.00, due 2024-03-15")
print(result["category"])        # invoice
print(result["fields"]["vendor"])  # Acme Corp
Notice the structure: every node is a single LLM call with a typed output, the branching condition is a Python
if
statement on a Pydantic field value, and no node decides what to do next by prompting the LLM for a plan. That is what makes it a workflow.
Implementing the Same Workflow in LangGraph
LangGraph's
StateGraph
makes the workflow structure explicit and enables streaming, checkpointing, and observability without changing the deterministic logic.
Workflow vs Agent: Side-by-Side Comparison
Dimension
Workflow
Agent
Execution graph
Fixed at build time
Determined at runtime
LLM call count
Bounded and predictable
Unbounded (depends on task)
Tool selection
Hardcoded per node
LLM picks from registry
Latency
Stable p99
Variable, can spike
Cost per run
Predictable
Can be 2–10× higher
Auditability
Full trace, reproducible
Harder to reproduce exactly
Handles novel input
Falls back to fixed branches
Adapts dynamically
Testability
Unit-testable node by node
Requires integration tests
Workflow Patterns and Their Fit
Different workflow patterns suit different problem shapes. Choosing the right pattern is a design decision separate from choosing workflow vs agent:
Prompt chaining
— each LLM call refines the output of the previous one. Use when quality improves by decomposing a complex task into sequential sub-tasks (e.g. draft → verify → polish).
Routing
— a classifier selects which downstream prompt or chain handles the input. Use when different input categories need different specialised prompts.
Parallelization
— multiple LLM calls run concurrently and their results are aggregated. Use when sub-tasks are independent and latency matters.
Orchestrator-workers
— a coordinator LLM dispatches to specialised worker chains. Use when work can be decomposed into typed sub-tasks with known handlers.
Evaluator-optimizer
— a generator and an evaluator loop until a quality threshold is met. Use for content, code, or structured output that benefits from iterative refinement with a fixed stopping criterion.
Testing Workflows: Node-Level Unit Tests

Because every node has typed inputs and outputs, workflow nodes are trivially unit-testable by mocking the LLM call. This is a major operational advantage over agents, where the test must cover the full decision loop.

import pytest
from unittest.mock import patch, MagicMock
from pydantic import BaseModel
from typing import Literal

# Test the classify node in isolation
def test_classify_node_returns_invoice():
    mock_result = DocClass(category="invoice", confidence=0.95)
    with patch.object(classify_chain, "invoke", return_value=mock_result):
        state = {"text": "Invoice #100 from Acme", "category": None, "confidence": None, "fields": None}
        result = node_classify(state)
    assert result["category"] == "invoice"
    assert result["confidence"] == 0.95

# Test routing logic without any LLM call
def test_route_by_category_invoice():
    state = {"text": "", "category": "invoice", "confidence": 0.9, "fields": None}
    assert route_by_category(state) == "invoice"

def test_route_by_category_other():
    state = {"text": "", "category": "other", "confidence": 0.5, "fields": None}
    assert route_by_category(state) == "other"

# Integration test: full pipeline with mocked LLM
@pytest.mark.parametrize("text,expected_category", [
    ("Invoice #100, due March 15", "invoice"),
    ("Contract between Party A and Party B", "contract"),
])
def test_full_workflow_integration(text, expected_category, mocker):
    # Mock classify step
    mocker.patch.object(
        classify_chain, "invoke",
        return_value=DocClass(category=expected_category, confidence=0.9)
    )
    # Mock extract steps
    if expected_category == "invoice":
        mock_fields = InvoiceFields(vendor="Acme", total_amount=1000.0, due_date="2024-03-15", line_items=[])
        mocker.patch.object(make_extract_chain(InvoiceFields), "invoke", return_value=mock_fields)
    result = triage_document(text)
    assert result["category"] == expected_category
    assert "fields" in result
Anti-Patterns: When Workflows Are a Poor Fit
Workflows are the wrong choice when:
The task requires open-ended tool use.

If the LLM needs to choose from a large tool registry based on what it discovers mid-execution (browsing the web, writing and running code, querying arbitrary databases), a workflow cannot enumerate the branches in advance.
The number of steps depends on the input.

Tasks like "research this topic until you have enough information" or "debug this code until the tests pass" require an unknown number of iterations. A workflow with a fixed iteration count will either under-do or over-do the work.
The domain is poorly understood.

If you cannot fully specify the graph — because you do not yet know all the edge cases or branch conditions — building a workflow means encoding incomplete knowledge. An agent's adaptive behavior is more forgiving during exploration.
The task is exploratory by nature.
Competitive analysis, investigative research, and root-cause analysis require the LLM to decide what to look at based on what it finds. These tasks have no pre-known step sequence.
Hybrid Architectures: Starting with Workflows

In practice, the boundary between workflows and agents is architectural rather than fundamental. A common pattern is to start with a workflow for the well-understood core path and embed a small agent only in the specific node where open-ended reasoning is needed:

from langgraph.prebuilt import create_react_agent
from langchain_core.tools import tool

# The research node uses a bounded agent internally
@tool
def search_docs(query: str) -> str:
    """Search internal knowledge base."""
    return f"Results for: {query}"  # real implementation uses a vector store

research_agent = create_react_agent(
    model=llm,
    tools=[search_docs],
    # Bound the agent: max 3 tool calls before it must return
    # (achieved by limiting recursion_limit in invoke config)
)

def node_research(state: TriageState) -> TriageState:
    # This node is an agent, but it lives inside a larger workflow
    result = research_agent.invoke(
        {"messages": [("human", f"Find relevant context for: {state['text']}")]},
        config={"recursion_limit": 6}  # 3 think + 3 tool calls
    )
    summary = result["messages"][-1].content
    return {"fields": {**state.get("fields", {}), "research": summary}}

# Insert the agent node into the workflow graph
graph.add_node("research", node_research)
graph.add_edge("extract_invoice", "research")
graph.add_edge("research", END)

This pattern — workflow outer shell, agent inner node — gives the outer system predictable structure and cost control while still leveraging the agent's flexibility in the one place it is actually needed. Start with as much workflow as possible and add agent nodes only where you cannot pre-specify the steps.
Workflow Observability and Debugging

One of the most practical advantages of workflows is that every node boundary is a natural instrumentation point. Because each node receives a typed state and returns a typed state, you can log the full input-output pair at every step with a single decorator, giving you complete execution traces without modifying node logic.

This trace pattern is impossible to apply mechanically to an agent because the agent's internal steps are not exposed as named Python functions — they occur inside the LLM's reasoning loop. Workflows give you full observability for free by virtue of their explicit node structure.
Cost Modeling and SLA Estimation

Because a workflow's LLM call count is fixed, you can build an exact cost model before deploying. Given the prompt template lengths and expected output sizes, you can compute tokens per node and multiply by price per token to get cost per run. This enables capacity planning, SLA estimation, and budget alerts that are simply not possible with agents.

from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class NodeCostModel:
    node_name: str
    avg_input_tokens: int
    avg_output_tokens: int
    model: str = "gpt-4o-mini"

    # Prices per 1M tokens (as of early 2024 — check current pricing)
    INPUT_PRICE = {"gpt-4o-mini": 0.15, "gpt-4o": 2.50}
    OUTPUT_PRICE = {"gpt-4o-mini": 0.60, "gpt-4o": 10.00}

    def cost_per_run(self) -> float:
        inp = (self.avg_input_tokens / 1_000_000) * self.INPUT_PRICE[self.model]
        out = (self.avg_output_tokens / 1_000_000) * self.OUTPUT_PRICE[self.model]
        return inp + out

# Model the triage pipeline
pipeline_nodes = [
    NodeCostModel("classify",         avg_input_tokens=300,  avg_output_tokens=30),
    NodeCostModel("extract_invoice",  avg_input_tokens=600,  avg_output_tokens=120),
]
total_cost_per_run = sum(n.cost_per_run() for n in pipeline_nodes)
monthly_cost_100k = total_cost_per_run * 100_000

print(f"Cost per run:  ${total_cost_per_run:.6f}")
print(f"100k runs/mo:  ${monthly_cost_100k:.2f}")
# Cost per run:  $0.000117
# 100k runs/mo:  $11.70

You can run the same model over a sample of real inputs during staging to calibrate the averages, then update the model before committing to a pricing tier or SLA. With an agent, cost per run depends on what the agent encounters in the input, making this analysis much harder.
Migration Path: From Workflow to Agent
Starting with a workflow and gradually introducing agent behavior is a lower-risk approach than starting with an agent and trying to constrain it. The migration typically follows this progression:
Pure workflow.
Every step fixed. No dynamic tool selection. Ship this first to establish baseline quality and cost.
Workflow with one agent node.
Replace the single most variable node with a bounded agent (max tool calls limited by
recursion_limit
). Monitor cost and latency impact.
Workflow with multiple agent nodes.
Add agent nodes one at a time as the domain analysis reveals more variability. Each new agent node should have its own cost and latency budget.
Full agent.
Only reach here if the entire task is too variable for any fixed structure. At this point you have enough operational data from the workflow phase to understand what the agent must handle.

This progression is reversible. If an agent node turns out to have stable enough behavior, you can replace it with a fixed workflow node to reduce cost. The decision to use a workflow is not permanent — it is an architectural choice you revisit as understanding of the domain improves.
Summary

Use a workflow when the task has a fixed execution graph, bounded LLM calls, and predictable input-output contracts. Workflows are faster, cheaper, more auditable, and easier to test than agents. The five signals that point to a workflow are: steps known in advance, fully specified output format, hard latency or cost constraints, auditability requirements, and a stable, well-understood domain. When some part of the task genuinely requires open-ended reasoning or dynamic tool selection, embed an agent node inside an otherwise workflow-structured system rather than converting the entire design to an agent.
